name: Reusing Highly Optimised IR in Dynamic Compilation
summary: Oracle Labs ECOOP 2025: cache and preserve highly optimised IR across recompilations in dynamic compilation systems. Reduces the repeated optimisation work that re-running the optimiser would do, at the cost of assumption-invalidation tracking and IR-version compatibility.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Dynamic compilers (JITs) typically discard optimised IR after producing machine code, then re-run the optimiser if the function needs recompilation due to deoptimisation, profile changes, or speculative invalidation. The Oracle Labs ECOOP 2025 paper presents techniques for **caching and reusing highly optimised IR** across these recompilations.

The payoff: the second compilation pass starts from the optimised IR of the first, skipping most of the optimisation work that produced it. Recompilations after deopt or speculation invalidation finish in a fraction of the time they would otherwise.

sources.label: Reusing Highly Optimized IR in Dynamic Compilation (ECOOP 2025)

sections.title: Trade-offs
sections.content:
The cost is in tracking what assumptions the cached IR depends on. Every speculative optimisation in the cached IR is conditional on a runtime assumption; if any of those assumptions invalidate, the cached IR is invalid for those parts. Tracking assumption dependencies precisely is non-trivial.

The second cost is IR versioning: the optimiser's IR format may change between runs (across compiler updates, across runtime restarts), so cached IR from one version may not be loadable by a later one. Production systems handle this with version tags and a fallback to recompilation when the cache is stale.

The trade-off pays off most when recompilation is frequent — speculative-deopt-heavy workloads, streaming workloads where profile changes trigger frequent re-specialisation, and warm-up tiers that re-optimise on additional profile data.
